We have modelled D. melanogaster and fission yeast, and the models are equivalent with very minor differences (eg. MIM complex in yeast is replaced by a functionally analogous system in metazoa). This is the set of terms required to describe all mitochondrial import pathways: - [x] 1. GO_NEW mitochondrial protein import pathway (is_ mitochondrial transport) Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol to their final destination in the mitochondria. - [ ] 2. GO_NEW ~mitochondrial outer membrane alpha helical protein insertion~ (1. In the image) alpha helical protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane Narrow synonym MIM pathway (different complex in higher eukaryotes) Narrow synonym: MTCH pathway Definition: The import of alpha helical and tail-anchored proteins from the cytosol and their insertion into the mitochondrial outer membrane. Note:In yeast, the insertase is the mitochondrial import (MIM) complex, composed of Mim1 and Mim2. In higher eukaryotes, the functional counterpart MTCH performs a similar role despite lacking sequence and structural homology, reflecting convergent evolution (PMID:40782022) Model: [[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69a0c46f00003239)][[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69a0c46f00003187)] - [ ] 3. Typically acting on single-pass transmembrane proteins with an N-terminal targeting sequence and an internal hydrophobic stop-transfer sequence which targets them for lateral insertion. Note: Some proteins utilize this pathway but can be further processed and released from the inner membrane into the IMS. Resident Ccs1 then recruits incoming Sod1 precursors by oxidative folding. A minor parallel route allows some Sod1 to enter the IMS independently of Ccs1, via Mia40 directly. In humans: CCS1 import is autocatalytic, with mature IMS-resident CCS1 acting as its own import receptor via oxidative folding. This reaction is ROS-sensitive -> mitochondrial oxidative stress directly increases IMS recruitment of both CCS1 and SOD1. Metalation and oxidative folding of Sod1: Sod1 requires copper and a zinc ion cofactors. Ccs1 directly mediates copper delivery to Sod1 and forms a transient intermolecular disulfide with Sod1 to facilitate copper transfer and disulfide bond formation simultaneously. Zinc incorporation is thought to occur independently of Ccs1, prior to the Ccs1 interaction. The mitochondrial version of superoxide dismutase is in the matrix not the IMS, and it isn't copper dependent (the cytosolic one is)...the mitochondrial one uses manganese as its cofactor ","createdAt":"2026-03-13T13:55:48Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"outdated","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4055236224","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xtjnz","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"actually claude says Sod1 (Cu/Zn-SOD) — the cytoplasmic form Primarily found in the cytoplasm, though a small fraction can also be present in the mitochondrial intermembrane space but I don't know if this is true for fission yeast where the cytosolic form hasn't been detected in the mitochondria","createdAt":"2026-03-13T13:57:28Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"outdated","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4055251443","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xuaHA","author":{"login":"hattrill"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":" my claude.ai says: In the mitochondrial IMS specifically, the same chemistry applies. CCS is imported and retained in the IMS (via the Mia40/Erv1 pathway in yeast, or self-mediated import in S. pombe/humans), where it loads Cu(I) into apoSOD1 and simultaneously introduces the intramolecular disulfide, maturing SOD1 for its antioxidant role against respiratory chain-derived superoxide. And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.

And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.
